markdown:
## HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) 1. **Stateless Protocol **: HTTP is **stateless**, meaning each request is independent and does not retain information about previous interactions. 2. **Request-Response Model **: Communication occurs through a **client-server model**, where the client sends a request and the server responds. 3. **Text-Based **: HTTP messages are **human-readable** and consist of methods like **GET **, **POST **, **PUT **, and **DELETE**. 4. **Port 80 **: By default, HTTP operates on **port 80**. HTTP **does not encrypt data**, making it vulnerable to eavesdropping. ## HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) 1. **Secure Version of HTTP **: HTTPS adds a layer of **encryption** using protocols like **SSL/TLS**. 2. **Data Integrity and Confidentiality **: Ensures that data is **encrypted** during transmission, protecting it from interception. 3. **Authentication **: Uses **digital certificates** to verify the identity of the server. 4. **Port 443 **: HTTPS operates on **port 443**. HTTPS is essential for **secure transactions**, such as online banking and shopping. ## HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) 1. **Markup Language **: HTML is used to **structure** web content using **tags** like

,

, and . 2. **Static Content **: Defines the **content** and **layout** of web pages but does not handle dynamic behavior. 3. **Platform Independent**: HTML files can be rendered by any web browser. HTML tags are enclosed in angle brackets, e.g.,

Title

. ## URL (Uniform Resource Locator) 1. **Web Address **: A URL specifies the **location** of a resource on the internet. 2. **Components **: Includes the **protocol** (e.g., https://), **domain** (e.g., www.example.com), and **path** (e.g., /index.html). 3. **Human-Readable**: Designed to be easily understood and remembered by users. A URL like https://www.example.com/about points to the "about" page on the "example.com" domain using HTTPS. ## XML (Extensible Markup Language) 1. **Self-Descriptive **: XML uses **tags** to describe data, making it both **human-readable** and **machine-readable**. 2. **Hierarchical Structure **: Data is organized in a **tree-like** structure with **nested elements**. 3. **Platform Independent **: XML is widely used for **data exchange** between different systems. An XML document might look like this: John Doe A ## XSLT (Extensible Stylesheet Language Transformations) 1. **Transformation Language **: XSLT is used to **transform** XML documents into other formats, such as HTML or plain text. 2. **Template-Based **: Uses **templates** to define how elements should be processed and displayed. 3. **Declarative Syntax **: Focuses on **what** should be done rather than **how** to do it. XSLT can convert an XML document into an HTML table for web display. ## JavaScript 1. **Client-Side Scripting **: JavaScript runs in the **browser **, enabling **dynamic** and **interactive** web pages. 2. **Event-Driven **: Responds to **events** like clicks, form submissions, and mouse movements. 3. **Versatile **: Can manipulate the **DOM **, validate forms, and communicate with servers using **AJAX**. A simple JavaScript function to display an alert: function greet() { alert("Hello, World!"); } ## CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) 1. **Styling Language **: CSS is used to define the **appearance** of HTML elements, including colors, fonts, and layouts. 2. **Separation of Concerns **: Keeps **style** separate from **content**, making web pages easier to maintain. 3. **Cascading Rules**: Styles can be applied at different levels (inline, internal, external), with a hierarchy determining which rules take precedence. CSS can change the color of all

elements to blue: h1 { color: blue; } ## Summary Table | Technology | Characteristics | | --- | --- | | HTTP | Stateless, text-based, operates on port 80 | | HTTPS | Encrypted, secure, operates on port 443 | | HTML | Markup language, structures content, static | | URL | Specifies resource location, human-readable | | XML | Self-descriptive, hierarchical, data exchange | | XSLT | Transforms XML, template-based, declarative | | JavaScript | Client-side scripting, dynamic, event-driven | | CSS | Styles HTML, separates content and design, cascading rules |
